title: 1. Getting Started
text: identity dataset contains main ranking question four sources identity anchor-ranking question known correct ordering. also includes survey weight s_weight. app_identity columns describe ranking question interest, anc_correct_identity indicates whether respondent answered anchor question correctly.
code: identity |>   select(     s_weight,     app_identity,     starts_with("app_identity_"),     anc_correct_identity   ) |>   head() #> # A tibble: 6 × 9 #>   s_weight app_identity app_identity_1 app_identity_2 app_identity_3 #>      <dbl> <chr>                 <dbl>          <dbl>          <dbl> #> 1    0.844 1423                      1              4              2 #> 2    0.886 1423                      1              4              2 #> 3    2.96  3412                      3              4              1 #> 4    0.987 1423                      1              4              2 #> 5    1.76  4132                      4              1              3 #> 6    0.469 3124                      3              1              2 #> # ℹ 4 more variables: app_identity_4 <dbl>, app_identity_recorded <chr>, #> #   app_identity_row_rnd <chr>, anc_correct_identity <dbl>

title: 1. Getting Started
text: imprr_direct function directly estimates bias-corrected quantities interest average ranks, pairwise ranking probabilities, top-k probabilities, marginal rank probabilities. first output summarizes estimated proportion random responses. second output contains several corrected ranking-based quantities interest. first pass, average ranks often easiest place start.
code: out_direct <- imprr_direct(   data = identity,   J = 4,   main_q = "app_identity",   anc_correct = "anc_correct_identity",   weight = "s_weight" ) out_direct$est_p_random #>        mean     lower     upper #> 1 0.3512825 0.3077923 0.3977214 out_direct$results |>   filter(qoi == "average rank") #> # A tibble: 4 × 6 #>   item           qoi          outcome              mean lower upper #>   <chr>          <chr>        <chr>               <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 app_identity_1 average rank Avg: app_identity_1  3.27  3.13  3.40 #> 2 app_identity_2 average rank Avg: app_identity_2  2.58  2.43  2.75 #> 3 app_identity_3 average rank Avg: app_identity_3  1.66  1.52  1.81 #> 4 app_identity_4 average rank Avg: app_identity_4  2.49  2.37  2.59

title: 1. Getting Started
text: imprr_weights function instead produces respondent-level weights can used downstream analyses. One output gives bias-correction weight assigned possible ranking profile. respondent-level output keeps original data appends weights column along unified ranking column.
code: out_weights <- imprr_weights(   data = identity,   J = 4,   main_q = "app_identity",   anc_correct = "anc_correct_identity",   weight = "s_weight" ) out_weights$rankings |>   select(ranking, weights) |>   head() #>   ranking   weights #> 1    1234 0.4413785 #> 2    1243 0.0000000 #> 3    1324 0.3402731 #> 4    1342 0.0000000 #> 5    1423 0.9819455 #> 6    1432 0.2721408 out_weights$results |>   select(weights, s_weight, app_identity, ranking) |>   head() #> # A tibble: 6 × 4 #>   weights s_weight app_identity ranking #>     <dbl>    <dbl> <chr>        <chr>   #> 1   0.982    0.844 1423         1423    #> 2   0.982    0.886 1423         1423    #> 3   1.32     2.96  3412         3412    #> 4   0.982    0.987 1423         1423    #> 5   1.14     1.76  4132         4132    #> 6   0.966    0.469 3124         3124

title: 2. Basic Setup
text: perform bias correction, data must call anchor ranking question. anchor question auxiliary ranking question looks similar target question, whose “correct” answer known researchers. example, identity responses anchor question asked respondents rank four nested units: household, neighborhood, city, state. responses included anc_identity_1, anc_identity_2, anc_identity_3, anc_identity_4. , correct answer assumed "1234". Based responses, code indicator variable (anc_correct_identity) takes 1 respondents offer correct answer 0 otherwise. following dataset, first third respondents provided incorrect answer anchor question, whereas rest provided correct answer.
code: identity[, c(paste0("anc_identity_", seq(4)), "anc_correct_identity")] |>   tail() #> # A tibble: 6 × 5 #>   anc_identity_1 anc_identity_2 anc_identity_3 anc_identity_4 #>            <dbl>          <dbl>          <dbl>          <dbl> #> 1              3              1              2              4 #> 2              1              2              3              4 #> 3              4              3              2              1 #> 4              1              2              3              4 #> 5              1              2              3              4 #> 6              1              2              3              4 #> # ℹ 1 more variable: anc_correct_identity <dbl>

what: Estimating the Proportion of Random Responses
title: 3. Correcting Bias in Ranking Data
text: identity dataset, given anc_correct_identity, raw proportion correctly incorrectly answered anchor question follows: 69.7% seen , however, likely upwardly biased estimate percentage non-random responses, must account respondents accidentally answering question correctly. unbiased estimate random responses, use unbiased_correct_prop. revised estimate non-random responses 68.4%. say, roughly 31.6% respondents randomly responding.
code: round(prop.table(table(identity$anc_correct_identity)) * 100, digits = 1) ##  ##    0    1  ## 30.3 69.7 identity$random_identity <- case_when(   identity$anc_correct_identity == 1 ~ 0,   TRUE ~ 1 )  unbiased_correct_prop(   sum(identity$random_identity == 0) / sum(!is.na(identity$random_identity)),   J = 4 ) ## [1] 0.6836776

title: 3. Correcting Bias in Ranking Data
text: rankingQ two primary functions perform bias correction. First, imprr_direct improves ranking data applying direct bias correction several classes quantities interest. apply bias correction, specify dataset (data), number items (J), prefix column names contain J items target ranking questions, prefix column names anchor ranking questions. survey weights available, can included specifying weight function. default, imprr_direct assumes target population set non-random respondents. researchers wish study entire population target group, additional arguments must specified, including population assumption. example, uniform preference assumption can specified follows: Similarly, contaminated sampling assumption can specified follows:
code: ## app_identity_1 indicates marginal rank for party ## app_identity_2 indicates marginal rank for religion ## app_identity_3 indicates marginal rank for gender ## app_identity_4 indicates marginal rank for race  # Perform bias correction out_direct <- imprr_direct(   data = identity,   ## Not strictly necessary if app_identity, the input for `main_q`,   ## is specified. In that case, will look for J if unspecified   J = 4,   ## automatically looks for   ## app_identity_1, app_identity_2, app_identity_3, app_identity_4   main_q = "app_identity",   anc_correct = "anc_correct_identity",   # setting to 10 only for our vignette   n_bootstrap = 10 ) ## No weight column supplied; using equal weights for all observations. # Bias correction for the entire population with the uniform assumption  out_direct_uniform <- imprr_direct(   data = identity,   J = 4,   main_q = "app_identity",   anc_correct = "anc_correct_identity",   population = "all",   assumption = "uniform",   n_bootstrap = 10 ) ## population = 'all' with assumption = 'uniform' implies no correction; ignoring anc_correct and p_random. ## No weight column supplied; using equal weights for all observations. # Bias correction for the entire population with contaminated sampling  out_direct_contaminated <- imprr_direct(   data = identity,   J = 4,   main_q = "app_identity",   anc_correct = "anc_correct_identity",   population = "all",   assumption = "contaminated",   n_bootstrap = 10 ) ## No weight column supplied; using equal weights for all observations.

what: Results: Estimated Proportion of Random Responses
title: 3. Correcting Bias in Ranking Data
text: first output imprr_direct estimated proportion random responses. vector est_p_random returns estimated proportion along lower upper ends corresponding 95% confidence interval.
code: # Estimated proportion of random responses with a 95% CI out_direct$est_p_random ##        mean     lower     upper ## 1 0.3158402 0.2875352 0.3451338

title: 3. Correcting Bias in Ranking Data
text: output bias-corrected estimates four classes ranking-based quantities, including average ranks pairwise ranking probabilities top-k ranking probabilities marginal ranking probabilities output tibble qoi stores estimated quantities corresponding 95% CIs. example, one can visualize result average ranks follows:

title: 3. Correcting Bias in Ranking Data
text: alternative method bias correction based idea inverse-probability weighting (IPW). imprr_weights improves ranking data computing bias correction weights, can used correct bias IPW framework. arguments previously used can used follows: default, imprr_weights assumes target population set non-random respondents. researchers wish study entire population target group, additional arguments must specified, including population assumption. example, uniform preference assumption can specified follows: Similarly, contaminated sampling assumption can specified follows:
code: # Perform bias correction out_weights <- imprr_weights(   data = identity,   J = 4,   main_q = "app_identity",   anc_correct = "anc_correct_identity" ) ## No weight column supplied; using equal weights for all observations. # Perform bias correction with the uniform preference assumption out_weights_uniform <- imprr_weights(   data = identity,   J = 4,   main_q = "app_identity",   anc_correct = "anc_correct_identity",   population = "all",   assumption = "uniform" ) ## population = 'all' with assumption = 'uniform' implies no correction; ignoring anc_correct and p_random. ## No weight column supplied; using equal weights for all observations. # Perform bias correction with the uniform preference assumption out_weights_contaminated <- imprr_weights(   data = identity,   J = 4,   main_q = "app_identity",   anc_correct = "anc_correct_identity",   population = "all",   assumption = "contaminated" ) ## No weight column supplied; using equal weights for all observations.

text: data input dataset ranking data. J number items ranking question. Defaults NULL, case inferred data. main_q Ranking question analyzed. `main_q` single column name unquoted symbol `app_identity`, function looks `app_identity_1`, `app_identity_2`, `app_identity_3`, . may also supply `main_q` directly character vector unquoted `c(...)` expression ranking columns `c(party, gender, race, religion)`. anc_correct Optional indicator passing anchor question. `NULL`, `p_random` used supplied; otherwise function defaults `p_random = 0` applies correction. population Choice target population non-random respondents (default) respondents. assumption Choice identifying assumption `population = ""`: `uniform` assumes random respondents uniform counterfactual preferences, `contaminated` assumes counterfactual preferences match non-random respondents. n_bootstrap Number bootstraps. Defaults 200. seed Seed set.seed reproducibility. weight name weight column `data`. Defaults `NULL`, uses equal weights. can also supplied numeric vector unquoted column name. verbose Indicator verbose output. Defaults FALSE. p_random Optional fixed proportion random/inattentive respondents. supplied, overrides `anc_correct` message shown provided.

what: rankingQ: Design-Based Methods for Improving Ranking Questions — rankingQ-package
title: rankingQ: Design-Based Methods for Improving Ranking Questions — rankingQ-package
text: Provides design-based methods reduce measurement error random responding survey ranking questions. Uses anchor-ranking items user-supplied random-response rates estimate corrected average ranks, marginal rank probabilities, pairwise comparisons, ranking distributions. Includes tools visualization, format conversion, simulation, diagnostic checks ranking data. Based Atsusaka Kim (2025) doi:10.1017/pan.2024.33 .

text: see behavior diagonalization occurred. survey software take recorded response translate true ranking respondent (observed ranking), providing recorded response. example, given reference choice set three items, , B, C, respondent may presented items order C, B, , may responded 3-2-1 recorded response. true order items , B, C, observed/true ranking 1-2-3. function takes C, B, 1-2-3 inputs returns 3-2-1.

title: Recover the Recorded Responses Given that Ranking Items were Randomized — recover_recorded_responses
code: ## This respondent's true ranking reported is A-B-C-D. ## However, the items were presented in the order B-A-D-C. ## Therefore, the respondent's recorded response is 2-1-4-3. recover_recorded_responses(true_order = "1234", "2143") ## Output: "2143" #> [1] "2143"  ## This respondent's true ranking is reported as D-C-B-A. ## However, the items were presented in the order A-B-C-D. ## Therefore, the respondent's recorded response is 4-3-2-1. recover_recorded_responses(true_order = "4321", "1234") ## Output: "4321" #> [1] "4321"  ## This respondent's true ranking is reported as C-A-D-B. ## However, the items were presented in the order D-C-B-A. ## Therefore, the respondent's recorded response is 3-1-4-2. recover_recorded_responses(true_order = "2413", "4321") ## Output: "3142" #> [1] "3142"  ## The same example using item labels directly. recover_recorded_responses("CADB", "DCBA") ## Output: "3142" #> [1] "3142"  ## You can also mix numeric rankings with labeled presentation order ## if the reference choice set is supplied explicitly. recover_recorded_responses("2413", "D|C|B|A", reference = c("A", "B", "C", "D")) #> [1] "3142"

title: Draw Samples from the Plackett-Luce Model — rpluce
text: data frame rankings t items n assessors.

title: Draw Samples from the Plackett-Luce Model — rpluce
text: Input: parameter \(\overrightarrow{\gamma} = (\gamma_1, \cdots, \gamma_m)\) Plackett-Luce model. remaining Plackett-Luce weights become zero earlier draws, remaining items sampled uniformly random rather ordered input position. Output: ranking \(R \\mathcal{L}(\mathcal{})\) \(\pi_{\overrightarrow{\gamma}}(\cdot)\) Plackett-Luce.    1: Let \(R = \emptyset\) \(= \mathcal{}\).    2: \(t = 1\) \(m\)    3:   Choose alternative \(a_{i_t}\) \(\)        probability proportional \(\gamma_{i_t}\).    4:   \(R \leftarrow R \succ a_{i_t}\)        \(\leftarrow \setminus \{ a_{i_t} \}\).    5: end    6: return \(R\).

title: Draw Samples from the Plackett-Luce Model — rpluce
code: rpluce(n = 10, t = 3, prob = c(0.5, 0.3, 0.2), seed = 123) #>    1st 2nd 3rd #> 1    c   a   b #> 2    a   c   b #> 3    b   c   a #> 4    a   b   c #> 5    a   b   c #> 6    a   b   c #> 7    c   a   b #> 8    b   c   a #> 9    a   c   b #> 10   a   c   b

title: rankingQ 0.2.0
text: First CRAN submission. package previously available GitHub version 0.1.0. New exported function rank_wider() converting long-format ranking data wide format (one column per item single pasted ranking string). imprr_direct(): anc_correct now optional. Users can instead supply p_random directly, rely anchor-based estimation non-random response rate. New exported convenience wrapper add_ipw_weights() returning original data augmented respondent-level IPW weights, option also keep ranking summaries. imprr_direct_rcpp(): correctly delegates imprr_direct() method = "fixed", forwarding population assumption inputs. rpluce(): remaining Plackett-Luce weights become zero earlier draws, remaining items now sampled uniformly random rather ordered input position. rank_longer(): stricter validation reference argument clearer handling single- vs multi-column input. Stronger input validation across package: weights must finite, non-negative, sum positive number; J must finite; permutation labels parsed consistently across compact, delimited, fixed-width formats (including J > 9). Permutation-label infrastructure rewritten support rankings arbitrary size. Documentation: switched \href{}{} external URLs; expanded examples \value{} sections.

title: rankingQ 0.1.0
text: Initial GitHub release (submitted CRAN). Implements design-based bias correction methods ranking survey questions, based Atsusaka Kim (2025) doi:10.1017/pan.2024.33. Core functions bias correction: imprr_direct(), imprr_direct_rcpp(), imprr_weights(). Rcpp backend bootstrap-based estimation. Visualization functions: plot_avg_ranking() plot_dist_ranking(). Utility functions ranking data manipulation: rank_longer(), item_to_rank(), recover_recorded_responses(), permn_augment(). Statistical testing uniformity_test(). Simulation support rpluce() Plackett-Luce sampling. Includes example dataset identity ranking survey.
